sanskrit:
युधामन्युश्च विक्रान्त उत्तमौजाश्च वीर्यवान्।
सौभद्रो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्व एव महारथाः॥
transliteration:
yudhāmanyuś ca vikrānta uttamaujāś ca vīryavān
saubhadro draupadeyāś ca sarva eva mahā-rathāḥ
literal_meaning: Yudhamanyu and the heroic, Uttamaujas and the valorous, the son of Subhadra and the sons of Draupadi—all indeed are great chariot-warriors.
contextual_meaning: Dhritarāshtra’s minister Sanjaya, cataloguing the Pāṇḍava forces, names the youngest generation—cousins and step-brothers who grew up together, now lined up as interchangeable military assets. The verse exposes the psychological anesthesia of war: beloved boys are reduced to martial labels, foreshadowing the grief that will erupt when the same roster becomes a casualty list. It is the moment when family identity is eclipsed by battlefield identity, a split that mirrors the ego’s habit of de-humanising the ‘other’ to justify violence.
psychological_state: A parent or leader who has packaged loved ones into résumé bullet-points to convince themselves that the coming sacrifice is ‘necessary’ and ‘heroic’, while a quiet voice inside already knows the names will soon be read at a funeral.
trigger_scenario: A manager presenting a project team of close friends to the board as ‘high-performance units’ knowing half will be laid off once the merger goes through, feeling both pride in their prowess and dread of the impending loss.
anchor_text: When we label people, we lose their humanity—and our own.
